item.title: Patti Smith Remembers Rachel Corrie, Sings "Peaceable Kingdom" at DN!'s 30th Anniversary Event
item.description: Over 2,000 people packed into the historic Riverside Church in New York on Monday to celebrate the 30th anniversary of Democracy Now! The program included a reading by legendary singer Patti Smith from her new memoir Bread of Angels, in which she remembered the U.S. peace activist Rachel Corrie, who was killed by Israeli forces in Gaza in 2003 while trying to protect Palestinian homes from destruction. Corrie inspired Smith’s song “Peaceable Kingdom,” which she performed alongside Tony Shanahan on guitar and her daughter Jesse Smith on piano.
item.source:markdown: Over 2,000 people packed into the historic Riverside Church in New York on Monday to celebrate the 30th anniversary of _Democracy Now!_ The program included a reading by legendary singer Patti Smith from her new memoir _Bread of Angels_, in which she remembered the U.S. peace activist Rachel Corrie, who was killed by Israeli forces in Gaza in 2003 while trying to protect Palestinian homes from destruction. Corrie inspired Smith’s song “Peaceable Kingdom,” which she performed alongside Tony Shanahan on guitar and her daughter Jesse Smith on piano.

item.title: Pentagon Whistleblower Criticizes "Bloodthirst" of Iran War, Says Hegseth Is Enabling War Crimes
item.description:
As the United States mobilizes thousands more troops for deployment to the Middle East, we speak with retired U.S. Air Force Master Sergeant Wes Bryant, who criticizes the “bloodthirst” of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran. Bryant led the Pentagon office for civilian harm assessment from 2024 to 2025, before the unit was dissolved under Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.

The “wholly illegal war” has been “carried out recklessly from the start and with little regard for the innocent,” Bryant tells Democracy Now! “Pete Hegseth has already directed the committing of war crimes. And unfortunately, our senior military leadership is bending the knee and carrying out whatever he tells them to do.”
item.source:markdown:
As the United States mobilizes thousands more troops for deployment to the Middle East, we speak with retired U.S. Air Force Master Sergeant Wes Bryant, who criticizes the “bloodthirst” of the U.S.-Israeli war against Iran. Bryant led the Pentagon office for civilian harm assessment from 2024 to 2025, before the unit was dissolved under Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth.
The “wholly illegal war” has been “carried out recklessly from the start and with little regard for the innocent,” Bryant tells _Democracy Now!_ “Pete Hegseth has already directed the committing of war crimes. And unfortunately, our senior military leadership is bending the knee and carrying out whatever he tells them to do.”
item.author: webdev@democracynow.org (Democracy Now!)

item.title: New DHS Head Markwayne Mullin Is "Trump Loyalist, Anti-immigrant, Incompetent": Rep. Delia Ramirez
item.description:
Markwayne Mullin was sworn in Tuesday as secretary of the Department of Homeland Security, replacing Kristi Noem, who was ousted earlier this month. Mullin has served as senator for Oklahoma since 2023 following a decade in the House of Representatives. He joins the Trump administration amid a partial government shutdown, with Democrats demanding reforms to immigration enforcement before fully funding DHS.

“This agency of terror needs to be dismantled,” says Congressmember Delia Ramirez of Illinois. “And whether it is Mullin, whether it is Noem, whether it’s Tom Homan, that agency is dangerous, and every American should say, 'Enough is enough.'”

Ramirez also comments on the birthright citizenship case before the Supreme Court, the U.S. fuel blockade against Cuba and more.
item.source:markdown:
Markwayne Mullin was sworn in Tuesday as secretary of the Department of Homeland Security, replacing Kristi Noem, who was ousted earlier this month. He joins the Trump administration amid a partial government shutdown, with Democrats demanding reforms to immigration enforcement before fully funding DHS.
“This agency of terror needs to be dismantled,” says Congressmember Delia Ramirez of Illinois. “And whether it is Mullin, whether it is Noem, whether it’s Tom Homan, that agency is dangerous, and every American should say, 'Enough is enough.'”
Ramirez also comments on the birthright citizenship case before the Supreme Court, the U.S. fuel blockade against Cuba and more.

item.title: MIDDLE EAST LIVE 25 March: All eyes on Strait of Hormuz; war is ‘out of control’, UN chief warns
item.description: It’s day 26 of war in the Middle East. The UN chief says with the conflict now totally out of control, diplomacy must prevail. Ongoing strikes in Israel and Iran have included intensifying Israeli attacks against Hezbollah targets in Lebanon, while some US troops are heading to the region, the Pentagon has confirmed. Meanwhile, Iran has told the UN maritime agency that the Strait of Hormuz remains open to “non-hostile” ships not associated with the US and Israel. UN News app users can follow coverage here.
item.source:markdown: It’s day 26 of war in the Middle East. The UN chief says with the conflict now totally out of control, diplomacy must prevail. item.title: U.S. offers plan for a ceasefire, but Iran says Washington in no position to negotiate
item.description: Iran has received an American 15-point plan for a ceasefire for the Iran war through intermediaries from Pakistan, officials in Islamabad said Wednesday. item.title: Canada is pitching its energy ambitions in Texas — can the oil and gas industry be convinced?
item.description: Though oil and gas industry executives at CERAWeek, a global energy conference in Houston, welcomed Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson’s enthusiasm, they still say they want to see considerable action before they can be sure Canada's federal government is serious about changing the country’s reputation as being reluctant to expand the energy sector, especially oil and natural gas.
item.source:markdown:
![A man in a suit speaks at a podium with red walls in the background.](https://i.cbc.ca/ais/2993af12-e439-4f61-9e81-7ea3f53e950d,1774397091882/full/max/0/default.jpg?im=Crop%2Crect%3D%28450%2C0%2C5237%2C2945%29%3BResize%3D%28620%29)
Though oil and gas industry executives at CERAWeek, a global energy conference in Houston, welcomed Natural Resources Minister Tim Hodgson’s enthusiasm, they still say they want to see considerable action before they can be sure Canada's federal government is serious about changing the country’s reputation as being reluctant to expand the energy sector, especially oil and natural gas.

item.title: Montreal saw record 12 frostbite amputations in 2025, but none this year. Here's why
item.description: Despite a long and cold winter, no frostbite amputations have been reported in Montreal so far this year compared to an annual average of about six. The drop in cases could be connected to the addition of hundreds of spots in temporary warming shelters around the city and the use of a new drug protocol to treat frostbite.
item.source:markdown:
![A man sitting on the street.](https://i.cbc.ca/ais/1.5894940,1766514050116/full/max/0/default.jpg?im=Crop%2Crect%3D%280%2C0%2C4995%2C2809%29%3BResize%3D%28620%29)
Despite a long and cold winter, no frostbite amputations have been reported in Montreal so far this year compared to an annual average of about six. The drop in cases could be connected to the addition of hundreds of spots in temporary warming shelters around the city and the use of a new drug protocol to treat frostbite.

item.title: Family of man killed in Ontario police shooting says he'd asked for help, reflecting 'gaps' in the system
item.description: A 39-year-old man was killed in an Ontario Provincial Police shooting this month after they responded to a call about a person in crisis. Here's what the family shared as the Special Investigations Unit (SIU) looks into how he died.

item.title: OpenAI shuts down Sora app amid rising concerns about deepfakes and consent
item.description: OpenAI is shutting down its social media app Sora, which went viral last fall as a place to share short-form videos generated by artificial intelligence, but also raised alarms in Hollywood and beyond about the dangers of letting people create AI videos that resulted in nonconsensual images and realistic deepfakes.
item.source:markdown:

item.title: Canada pauses removals of inadmissible individuals to several Middle East countries

item.title: As Trump Deploys ICE Agents to Airports, TSA Agents Continue to Go Without Pay: AFGE Union Steward
item.description:
The partial government shutdown over funding for the Department of Homeland Security has caused major staffing shortages within the Transportation Security Administration, an agency under DHS that handles airport security procedures created in response to the September 11, 2001, attacks on the World Trade Center. TSA officers have not been paid for over a month, leading to hundreds of resignations and leaves of absence. As airports are hit with significant delays, Cameron Cochems, the vice president of a union representing thousands of fellow TSA workers, urges travelers to use their time waiting to call their elected representatives “to fund the TSA so we can get our paychecks and we can get back to work.”

To fill staffing shortages, DHS has deployed Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents to U.S. airports. This comes as ICE is under fire for a hiring surge with minimal vetting and training for new officers. “It really feels like they’re a Band-Aid over a gaping wound. Our officers, they’re not getting paid, and having people that come in that are getting paid just feels like an insult,” says Cochems, who adds that the ultimate goal of the Republican Party is to eliminate union protections and privatize the TSA.
item.source:markdown:
The partial government shutdown over funding for the Department of Homeland Security has caused major staffing shortages within the Transportation Security Administration, an agency under DHS that handles airport security procedures created in response to the September 11, 2001, attacks on the World Trade Center. TSA officers have not been paid for over a month, leading to hundreds of resignations and leaves of absence. As airports are hit with significant delays, Cameron Cochems, the vice president of a union representing thousands of fellow TSA workers, urges travelers to use their time waiting to call their elected representatives “to fund the TSA so we can get our paychecks and we can get back to work.”
To fill staffing shortages, DHS has deployed Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents to U.S. airports. This comes as ICE is under fire for a hiring surge with minimal vetting and training for new officers. “It really feels like they’re a Band-Aid over a gaping wound. Our officers, they’re not getting paid, and having people that come in that are getting paid just feels like an insult,” says Cochems, who adds that the ultimate goal of the Republican Party is to eliminate union protections and privatize the TSA.
item.author: webdev@democracynow.org (Democracy Now!)

item.title: Bruce Springsteen Performs "Streets of Minneapolis" at Democracy Now!'s 30th Anniversary
item.description: Democracy Now! celebrated its 30th anniversary at the historic Riverside Church in New York City with live performances by guests including musician Bruce Springsteen. Springsteen performed his new song, “Streets of Minneapolis,” about the killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti by immigration enforcement agents in Minneapolis and spoke about his upcoming tour, which begins in Minneapolis next week. Watch the full event here.

item.title: "Under the Rubble": Pulitzer Prize Winner Reads Poem About Gaza at Democracy Now!'s 30th Anniversary
item.description: Democracy Now! celebrated its 30th anniversary at the historic Riverside Church in New York City with live performances by guests including the Pulitzer Prize-winning Palestinian poet Mosab Abu Toha. Mosab Abu Toha read his poem “Under the Rubble” and spoke about his experience surviving Israel’s genocide of Gaza, which killed 30 members of his extended family. Watch the full event here.

item.title: "Israel First": Ex-Israeli Negotiator Daniel Levy Says Netanyahu Led Trump into Illegal Iran War
item.description: “This is about how far Israel can extend its dominion, how much of a hard-power, dominant hegemon it can be in the region.” Daniel Levy of the U.S./Middle East Project says that U.S. involvement in the ongoing war on Iran is being driven by Israel’s expansionist ambitions in the Middle East. “Israel is still on the impunity high from its Gaza genocide, which has led us here.” Levy, a former Israeli peace negotiator, also provides analysis of current U.S., Israeli and Iranian wartime strategy, as well as potential ceasefire negotiations and Israel’s settler and soldier-backed ethnic cleansing of the occupied West Bank. “I think this will ultimately end very badly for Israel and generate tremendous blowback. But in the meantime, it is again the Palestinians bearing the brunt.”
item.source:markdown: “This is about how far Israel can extend its dominion, how much of a hard-power, dominant hegemon it can be in the region.” Daniel Levy of the U.S./Middle East Project says that U.S. involvement in the ongoing war on Iran is being driven by Israel’s expansionist ambitions in the Middle East. “Israel is still on the impunity high from its Gaza genocide, which has led us here.” Levy, a former Israeli peace negotiator, also provides analysis of current U.S., Israeli and Iranian wartime strategy, as well as potential ceasefire negotiations and Israel’s settler and soldier-backed ethnic cleansing of the occupied West Bank. “I think this will ultimately end very badly for Israel and generate tremendous blowback. But in the meantime, it is again the Palestinians bearing the brunt.”

item.title: Smith-Carney pipeline deal to miss early deadlines, premier says
item.description: The first set of deadlines included in the energy and climate deal struck between Prime Minister Mark Carney and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith are not expected to be met, showing early challenges with the agreement that includes pursuing a new export pipeline from Alberta to the West Coast.
item.source:markdown:

The first set of deadlines included in the energy and climate deal struck between Prime Minister Mark Carney and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith are not expected to be met, showing early challenges with the agreement that includes pursuing a new export pipeline from Alberta to the West Coast.

item.title: 2,000 happily ever afters: Ontario woman attempts to sell huge collection of Harlequin paperbacks
item.description: Book collector Krystin Golden of Windsor says the Harlequin Intrigue line of novels isn't just about romance, and that's why she has spent decades accumulating titles. But now she'd like to sell all 2,000 of them as a single lot.
item.source:markdown:
![A woman kneeling in among shelves filled with romance novels.](https://i.cbc.ca/ais/5ef9a4f9-ace9-407d-a7d1-453d11fe9639,1774053220589/full/max/0/default.jpg?im=Crop%2Crect%3D%280%2C378%2C4032%2C2268%29%3BResize%3D%28620%29)
Book collector Krystin Golden of Windsor says the Harlequin Intrigue line of novels isn't just about romance, and that's why she has spent decades accumulating titles. But now she'd like to sell all 2,000 of them as a single lot.

item.title: First Nation medical transportation program in Thunder Bay, Ont., axed over lack of funding
item.description: The Sioux Lookout First Nations Health Authority (SLFNHA) has announced it is suspending its medical transportation program in Thunder Bay, Ont., due to a lack of funding from Indigenous Services Canada and the Non-Insured Health Benefits program. Here's what the change means for people who rely on the service and the more than 20 staff members who have been laid off.
item.source:markdown:
![A line of vans is seen in a parking lot.](https://i.cbc.ca/ais/9e589119-8308-4809-9b3b-2a940b5bf9fe,1774298385310/full/max/0/default.jpg?im=Crop%2Crect%3D%280%2C212%2C3512%2C1975%29%3BResize%3D%28620%29)
The Sioux Lookout First Nations Health Authority (SLFNHA) has announced it is suspending its medical transportation program in Thunder Bay, Ont., due to a lack of funding from Indigenous Services Canada and the Non-Insured Health Benefits program. Here's what the change means for people who rely on the service and the more than 20 staff members who have been laid off.

item.title: How the RCMP spied on Indigenous organizations — and how we broke the story
item.description: Today, we begin publishing a series of exclusive stories that expose a sweeping RCMP program of surveillance, disruption and infiltration of Indigenous organizations between 1968 and 1982. Though long suspected by Indigenous leaders across Canada, the scope and scale of the spy program has remained hidden behind walls of government secrecy, until now.
item.source:markdown:
![A man and a woman examine documents at a desk.](https://i.cbc.ca/ais/0b6cf587-da22-46c4-aa72-bb2fc6e6c6da,1773923248401/full/max/0/default.jpg?im=Crop%2Crect%3D%280%2C27%2C2000%2C1125%29%3BResize%3D%28620%29)
Today, we begin publishing a series of exclusive stories that expose a sweeping RCMP program of surveillance, disruption and infiltration of Indigenous organizations between 1968 and 1982. Though long suspected by Indigenous leaders across Canada, the scope and scale of the spy program has remained hidden behind walls of government secrecy, until now.

item.title: Ultimatums, diplomacy and a trip to Graceland as Trump eyes a deal with Iran
item.description: America may be a nation at war, but the president's activities have been a mix of diplomacy and diversions - with the occasional swing toward the surreal.
item.source:markdown: America may be a nation at war, but the president's activities have been a mix of diplomacy and diversions - with the occasional swing toward the surreal.

item.title: EU, Australia reach agreement on long-awaited trade deal
item.description: The European Union and Australia have signed a trade ⁠deal marking the culmination of years of negotiations, as Europe seeks to diversify its export markets and expand ties beyond its traditional partners.
item.source:markdown: The European Union and Australia have signed a trade ⁠deal marking the culmination of years of negotiations, as Europe seeks to diversify its export markets and expand ties beyond its traditional partners.

item.title: Colombia military plane crash kills 66, four missing
item.description: The death toll in the crash of a Colombian Air Force plane ⁠nearly doubled to 66 as authorities continued to recover bodies from the wreckage.
item.source:markdown: The death toll in the crash of a Colombian Air Force plane ⁠nearly doubled to 66 as authorities continued to recover bodies from the wreckage.

item.title: Dáil votes to cut excise on petrol, diesel from midnight
item.description: The Dáil has voted to cut excise by 15 cent per litre on petrol and 20 cent per litre on diesel from midnight.
item.source:markdown: The Dáil has voted to cut excise by 15 cent per litre on petrol and 20 cent per litre on diesel from midnight.

item.title: Fuel excise cuts among measures to tackle surge in prices
item.description: The Government has published its proposals to cut excise duty (VAT inclusive) on diesel by 20 cent and by 15 cent on petrol until end of May.
item.source:markdown: The Government has published its proposals to cut excise duty (VAT inclusive) on diesel by 20 cent and by 15 cent on petrol until end of May.

item.title: 70% have never changed health insurance provider - study
item.description: Seven out of ten people with a health insurance policy have never changed their provider or plan, according to new research from the Health Insurance Authority.
item.source:markdown: Seven out of ten people with a health insurance policy have never changed their provider or plan, according to new research from the Health Insurance Authority.

item.title: Quarter of new student beds to come from digs in strategy
item.description: The Government will use public-private partnerships and spare bedrooms in private homes to create an additional 42,000 beds for students over the next nine years.
item.source:markdown: The Government will use public-private partnerships and spare bedrooms in private homes to create an additional 42,000 beds for students over the next nine years.

item.title: Major concern over pace of child homelessness - watchdog
item.description: The Children's Rights Alliance has expressed major concern over the pace of child and family homelessness, when matched against the construction of social housing.
item.source:markdown: The Children's Rights Alliance has expressed major concern over the pace of child and family homelessness, when matched against the construction of social housing.
